original_hypothesis.title: Withdrawal Epoch Rollover Rounding Creates Per-User/Epoch-Level Accounting Divergence
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In executeWithdrawal, sharesToFulfill is computed as (pendingAmount * sharesFulfilled / sharesWithdrawn), rounding down. The per-user sharesRemaining = amount - sharesToFulfill is thus rounded up. Similarly, withdrawnBasis uses the same rounding pattern. The aggregate rollover from fulfillWithdrawals uses exact subtraction at the epoch level, but per-user rollovers can sum to more than the epoch-level rollover. This mirrors the deposit rollover issue (TECH-H003) and causes the last withdrawal executor per epoch to receive fewer assets than their pro-rata share.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Same rounding mismatch as deposits but in the withdrawal path. Each user's unfulfilled shares slightly exceed the epoch-level allocation due to rounding, causing the sum of per-user claims in the next epoch to exceed the epoch's total.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sharesToFulfill rounds down per user; sharesRemaining rounds up; sum of per-user rollovers exceeds epoch-level rollover from fulfillWithdrawals

preconditions: Partial withdrawal fulfillment with multiple users

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-user rollover not synchronized with epoch-level rollover totals
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reconciliation mechanism between per-user and epoch-level withdrawal totals

analysis_questions: Does the discrepancy cause actual asset loss for the last executor?
Can the discrepancy cause an underflow in pro-rata calculations?
How does this interact with the basis rounding in the same function?
validation_requirements: Test with 10+ users and partial fulfillment
Sum all per-user rollovers and compare to epoch-level total
Verify last executor's pro-rata share is reduced

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 5 users each have 101 shares in withdrawal. 50 out of 505 total fulfilled. Each user's sharesToFulfill = 101*50/505 = 10 (rounded). sharesRemaining = 91 each. Sum = 455. But epoch-level rollover = 505-50 = 455. Exact here. With different numbers (e.g., 3 users at 100, 150 fulfilled out of 300): user1 = 100*150/300 = 50, remaining 50. All exact. Need to find a case where it diverges. With 3 users at 33: sharesToFulfill = 33*50/99 = 16 each. Remaining = 17 each. Sum = 51. Epoch rollover = 99-50=49. Divergence of 2!
prerequisites: Multiple users with partial fulfillment yielding non-exact division

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited per-epoch dust; compounds over protocol lifetime; last executor bears disproportionate loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Last withdrawal executor per partially-fulfilled epoch

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing multi-user partial withdrawal rollover rounding test

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by summing per-user withdrawal queues vs epoch-level state

validation_result.mechanism: Per-user rounding divergence from epoch-level rollover accounting
validation_result.impact_summary: Sum of per-user withdrawal rollovers exceeds epoch-level rollover due to per-user floor rounding in sharesToFulfill. This causes the last user(s) to potentially be unable to execute withdrawals when reservedWithdrawalAssets is insufficient.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: In fulfillWithdrawals, the epoch-level rollover is computed exactly: sharesRemaining = epochState.sharesWithdrawn - sharesToFulfill. In executeWithdrawal, each user's sharesToFulfill rounds down: floor(amount * sharesFulfilled / sharesWithdrawn), and sharesRemaining = amount - sharesToFulfill rounds up. Since sum(floor(a_i * B / A)) <= B when sum(a_i) = A, we get sum(per-user sharesRemaining) >= epoch-level sharesRemaining. The divergence equals the number of users experiencing non-exact division, up to one unit per user. These excess shares are written to queuedWithdrawal[user][epoch+1].amount but NOT reflected in withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn. When the next epoch is fulfilled and users execute, they collectively claim more shares than the epoch knows about. This causes sum(assetsToWithdraw) > assetsReceived for that epoch, potentially causing the last executor's reservedWithdrawalAssets subtraction to underflow/revert.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the code. fulfillWithdrawals (line ~436) sets sharesRemaining via exact subtraction and writes it to the next epoch's sharesWithdrawn (line ~448). executeWithdrawal (line ~271) computes per-user sharesToFulfill with floor division, yielding per-user sharesRemaining that individually round up. These per-user amounts are added to queuedWithdrawal[user][epoch+1].amount (line ~289) without updating withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn. The concrete example: 3 users each with 33 shares, sharesFulfilled=50, sharesWithdrawn=99. Per-user sharesToFulfill=floor(33*50/99)=16, sharesRemaining=17 each. Sum=51. Epoch-level rollover=99-50=49. Divergence=2.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Per-user sharesToFulfill rounds down via integer division, so sharesRemaining rounds up. These per-user rollovers are added to queuedWithdrawal[user][epoch+1].amount without updating epoch-level withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn, causing sum(per-user amounts) > epoch-level sharesWithdrawn.

Epoch-level rollover uses exact subtraction (sharesWithdrawn - sharesToFulfill). This value is written to withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn and is never updated when per-user rollovers are computed in executeWithdrawal. The per-user sum exceeds this value.

calling_sequence: 1. Manager calls fulfillWithdrawals(sharesToFulfill=50, ...) with 3 users each having 33 shares (total 99)
4. User1 calls executeWithdrawal: sharesToFulfill=floor(33*50/99)=16, sharesRemaining=17, rolls 17 to epoch+1
5. User2 calls executeWithdrawal: sharesToFulfill=floor(33*50/99)=16, sharesRemaining=17, rolls 17 to epoch+1
6. User3 calls executeWithdrawal: sharesToFulfill=floor(33*50/99)=16, sharesRemaining=17, rolls 17 to epoch+1
7. Sum of per-user rollovers to epoch+1: 51. But withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn=49. Divergence=2.
8. When epoch+1 is fulfilled and executed, users collectively claim assets proportional to 51 shares but epoch only accounts for 49
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: fulfillWithdrawals sets exact epoch-level rollover (line ~436). Each executeWithdrawal computes per-user rollover with floor division (line ~271), yielding ceil-rounded remainders (line ~282). Per-user rollovers are added to queuedWithdrawal[user][epoch+1].amount (line ~289) without updating withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn. In the next epoch, pro-rata calculations use the understated sharesWithdrawn as denominator, causing each user's sharesToFulfill to be inflated relative to what was actually allocated. The total assets claimed exceed assetsReceived, draining reservedWithdrawalAssets beyond what was allocated for that epoch.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The defect is inherent in the arithmetic logic, not deployment-dependent.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Concrete example: 3 users each request withdrawal of 33 shares in epoch E (total sharesWithdrawn=99). Manager fulfills 50 shares. Epoch-level rollover: 99-50=49 shares to epoch E+1. Per-user: floor(33*50/99)=16 fulfilled, 17 remaining each. Sum of per-user rollovers=51. Divergence=2 shares. When epoch E+1 is fully fulfilled (sharesFulfilled=49), each user's sharesToFulfill=floor(17*49/49)=17. Total claimed=51 shares worth of assets, but only 49 shares worth of assets were reserved. The last user to execute faces potential revert on reservedWithdrawalAssets underflow, or if other epochs' reserves cover it, those other epochs' users lose out.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The divergence is bounded by the number of users experiencing non-exact division per epoch (up to 1 share per user per epoch). For dust-level amounts this is minimal, but it compounds across epochs with partial fulfillment. Over the protocol's lifetime with many users and partial fulfillments, the accumulated divergence means: (1) sum(queuedWithdrawal[*][e].amount) > withdrawalEpochState[e].sharesWithdrawn for rolled-over epochs, violating INV-ECON-005; (2) the last executor per epoch may receive fewer assets or face a revert; (3) reservedWithdrawalAssets can be drained beyond what was allocated. This is a real accounting defect even if the per-epoch impact is small.
recommended_mitigations: Track per-user rollover at the epoch level: when executeWithdrawal rolls over shares to epoch+1, also increment withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn by sharesRemaining and withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1] basis equivalent
Alternatively, use a single-step calculation that ensures sum(per-user sharesToFulfill) == epoch-level sharesFulfilled, e.g., by giving the last user the residual instead of rounding independently
Add an invariant check that sum(queuedWithdrawal[*][e].amount) <= withdrawalEpochState[e].sharesWithdrawn, reverting if violated
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy HypoVault with a mock accountant. 2. Have 3 users each requestWithdrawal of 33 shares (total 99). 3. Manager calls fulfillWithdrawals with sharesToFulfill=50. 4. All 3 users call executeWithdrawal. 5. Verify withdrawalEpochState[epoch+1].sharesWithdrawn=49 but sum of queuedWithdrawal[user][epoch+1].amount=51. 6. Manager fulfills epoch+1 fully (sharesFulfilled=49). 7. All 3 users execute epoch+1. 8. Verify the third user's execution reverts due to insufficient reservedWithdrawalAssets, or total assets distributed exceed assetsReceived.
uncertainties: Whether the reservedWithdrawalAssets from other epochs could mask the divergence in practice
The exact magnitude of compounding across many epochs depends on user distribution and fulfillment ratios
Whether the basis divergence (same rounding pattern on withdrawnBasis) causes additional performance fee miscalculation
additional_testing_needed: Multi-user partial fulfillment test summing all per-user rollovers vs epoch-level total
Multi-epoch chaining test where divergence compounds across 5+ partial fulfillment epochs
Test with large number of users (100+) to measure maximum divergence per epoch
Test the last executor scenario to confirm revert on reservedWithdrawalAssets underflow
